• The private sector's increased involvement is crucial for accelerating the modernization of India's armed forces and fostering innovation.
• Highlights the government's push for greater participation of private industry in strategic defense production.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Security challenges and their management in border areas
prelimsPoints: First private sector missile production facility inaugurated in India.
Part of 'Make in India' in defense.
Aims to reduce import dependence for missile systems.
Boosts domestic defense manufacturing.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of private sector participation in India's defense manufacturing for achieving self-reliance and strategic autonomy.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in fostering a robust defense industrial base in India.
Examine how such initiatives contribute to job creation, technological advancement, and national security.
GS3.imageDescription: A sleek, modern missile being assembled inside a high-tech factory.
GS3.trivia: India is among the top global importers of defense equipment, and the 'Make in India' initiative aims to reverse this trend by promoting domestic manufacturing and reducing import dependency.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Security
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Indigenization of technology and developing new technology.

GS3.headline: India's Renewable Energy Capacity Crosses 200 GW Milestone
GS3.explainer:
• India's total installed renewable energy capacity, excluding large hydro, has officially crossed the 200 Gigawatt (GW) mark.
• This milestone signifies a major step towards achieving India's ambitious climate targets and energy transition goals.
• Solar and wind power constitute the largest share of this capacity, driven by supportive government policies and declining technology costs.
• The increased renewable capacity enhances energy security, reduces carbon emissions, and creates green employment opportunities.
• Further investments are planned to accelerate the transition away from fossil fuels and meet the 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity target by 2030.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
GS3.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
prelimsPoints: India's renewable energy capacity (excl. large hydro) crossed 200 GW.
Solar and wind power are major contributors.
Aims for 500 GW non-fossil fuel capacity by 2030.
Managed by MNRE.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of India crossing the 200 GW renewable energy capacity milestone for its energy security and climate goals.
Analyze the key drivers and policy interventions that have facilitated the growth of renewable energy in India.
Examine the challenges in integrating large-scale renewable energy into the national grid and strategies to overcome them.
GS3.imageDescription: A vast field of solar panels stretching into the horizon with wind turbines in the background.
GS3.trivia: India aims to achieve 50% of its energy requirements from renewable energy by 2030 and reduce the carbon intensity of its economy by 45% by 2030 from 2005 levels.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Environment
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Energy resources, renewable energy targets.

GS3.headline: Urban Local Bodies to Get Boost for Scientific Waste Management with New Scheme
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs announced a new scheme to support Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) in adopting scientific and sustainable waste management practices.
• The scheme provides financial assistance, technical expertise, and capacity building for waste segregation, processing, and disposal facilities.
• Focus on promoting circular economy principles, waste-to-energy projects, and reducing landfill dependency.
• Aims to improve urban sanitation, public health, and environmental quality across Indian cities.
• Encourages public-private partnerships and community participation in achieving 'Swachh Bharat Abhiyan' goals.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
GS3.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: New scheme for Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) on waste management.
Focus on scientific waste segregation, processing, disposal.
Promotes circular economy and waste-to-energy projects.
Aims to achieve Swachh Bharat Abhiyan goals.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges of urban waste management in India and the need for scientific approaches.
Analyze the key features of the new scheme and its potential impact on improving urban sanitation and environmental quality.
Examine the role of technology, public awareness, and private sector involvement in achieving sustainable waste management goals.
GS3.imageDescription: A clean urban street with separate bins for waste segregation, symbolizing effective waste management.
GS3.trivia: The 'Swachh Bharat Abhiyan' (Clean India Mission) was launched in 2014 by the Government of India to achieve universal sanitation coverage and promote cleanliness.

prelimsSyllabus.subject: Environment
prelimsSyllabus.topic: Waste management, environmental degradation.
